text.title: OKR Structure: Objectives, Key Results, and Scoring
text.summary: Defines what makes a valid Objective and Key Result, the scoring scale, and the structural rules (3-5 O, 3-5 KR each).

rule.statement: Objectives must be qualitative, inspiring, time-bound, and memorable. They must not contain numbers. Any Objective that contains a percentage or count is a Key Result in disguise.
rule.rationale: Objectives set direction; Key Results measure progress toward it. Mixing them destroys the separation that makes OKRs useful — if the Objective already contains the number, the KR adds nothing.

rule.statement: Limit to 3-5 Objectives per quarter. More than 5 is a strategic failure — everything is important means nothing is. Solo creators should limit to 1 Objective with 3 KRs.
rule.rationale: Focus is the primary value OKRs provide. An OKR set with 8 Objectives tells the team to work on everything, which is indistinguishable from having no OKRs.

example.note: Objective: "Delight early adopters and earn their advocacy." Qualitative, inspiring, does not contain a number, clearly time-bound to the quarter.
Objective: "Increase NPS to 50." This is a Key Result. The Objective should be "Build a product users love and recommend."

rule.statement: Key Results must be outcomes, not outputs. Ban the verbs ship/launch/build/release/create/design/implement/deploy at the start of any KR. KRs starting with these verbs are tasks, not outcomes.
rule.rationale: KRs measure whether the Objective was achieved, not whether work was done. "Ship feature X" can be 100% complete while the Objective fails entirely.

rule.statement: Every KR requires a baseline number stated on the day the OKRs are committed. No baseline equals no commitment. If the metric is not yet instrumented, write "TO BE INSTRUMENTED by [owner] by [date]" — never invent a baseline number.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline, "improve from X to Y" is undefined. Teams with no baseline cannot track progress and will report subjective completion at quarter end.

rule.statement: Target confidence at 70% (stretch but achievable). KRs with 100% confidence are sandbagged. KRs with 30% confidence are fantasy. Score 0.7 as ideal; 0.0-0.3 = failed, 0.4-0.6 = progress, 0.7-1.0 = success.
rule.rationale: The 70% target creates psychological safety for ambitious KRs. If hitting 100% is expected, teams anchor to safe targets. If 70% is the norm, teams stretch.

rule.statement: Never couple OKRs to compensation. Any KR linked to individual bonuses will be sandbagged within one quarter. OKRs are learning tools, not performance ratings.
rule.rationale: Compensation coupling converts stretch goals into low-risk targets and destroys the candor required for honest quarterly retrospectives.
